name: On surface around Rich Hill
short_description: Public BLM land near Prescott with surface gold and quartz specimens. Easy day-trip rockhounding with no permit required and a 25 lb daily collection limit.
description:
Rich Hill sits in the heart of Arizona's historic gold mining country, where the transition between the Basin and Range province and the Colorado Plateau creates excellent mineral-collecting opportunities. This BLM-managed area around the old Rich Hill mining district offers surface-accessible specimens of gold, quartz, and feldspar without the need for permits or special fees. The moderate elevation and nearby drainage systems mean you can explore relatively exposed hillsides where weathering has exposed mineralized quartz veins and associated metals. Most collectors find the area rewarding for both learning and casual weekend collecting, with good parking access and straightforward walking terrain.

The geology here is dominated by Proterozoic granitic bedrock that has been heavily mineralized by ancient hydrothermal systems related to regional extension and faulting. Quartz veins cutting through the granitic host rock often carry fine gold, visible under magnification or in the field on fresh breaks. Feldspar crystals from the granitic country rock are abundant, and mica flakes add glitter to many specimens. The area's mining history - dating back to the late 1800s - confirms the presence of economically meaningful gold concentrations, though most surface collecting yields small quantities suitable for personal collections or educational purposes.

Visitors should expect open, semi-arid landscape with scattered juniper and pinyon pine at this 5,196-foot elevation. Parking areas near access points are generally adequate for day-use groups. The moderate slope means some walking uphill is necessary to reach the best collecting zones, but the terrain is not technical or dangerous for hikers of average fitness. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and tools suitable for breaking samples from weathered quartz outcrops.
history: Rich Hill lies within the Hassayampa Mining District, one of Arizona's oldest and most productive gold regions. Proterozoic granitic intrusions were hydrothermally altered during regional extension events, creating quartz-hosted gold veins that attracted prospectors from the 1860s onward and sustained significant hard-rock mining through the early 1900s. Today the old mine workings and exposed mineralized zones make it an excellent location for surface specimen collecting.
tips: Focus your search on exposed quartz outcrops and the eroded hillsides where gravity and water have concentrated surface material - these areas are marked by white or pale cream-colored quartz fragments. Use a small magnifying glass (10x or higher) to inspect quartz samples for visible gold flakes, which often appear as tiny bright specks in fractured quartz. Bring a small hammer and cold chisel to carefully break samples from in-place quartz veins rather than collecting loose rocks, as this gives you cleaner specimens and better odds of finding visible gold. Scout the area after recent rains when freshly exposed material is most visible.
terrain_notes: Moderate sloping terrain at 5,196 feet elevation with typical Arizona high-desert vegetation including juniper and pinyon pine. Walking distances of 0.5 to 2 miles from parking areas to prime collecting zones. Footing is uneven with scattered loose rock and occasional small wash gullies; sturdy hiking boots are recommended. No wheelchair or ADA accessibility. Exposure to sun and wind is significant - plan for full sun protection.
best_season: October - April. Winter months offer cooler temperatures and lower heat stress while working outdoors. Summer temperatures regularly exceed 85 degrees F at this elevation, making midday collecting uncomfortable. Spring (March-April) and fall (September-October) shoulder seasons are also acceptable if you start early and finish by afternoon.
written_directions: From Prescott, take Highway 89 south approximately 8 miles toward Yavapai. Turn east on Forest Road 52 or similar BLM access road toward the Rich Hill area (confirm current access at Prescott BLM office). Continue on unpaved road for 3-4 miles, watching for small parking pullouts and old mining road entrances. The GPS coordinates 34.172805, -112.710444 will guide you to the primary collecting area. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour total drive time from central Prescott.
road_conditions: Unpaved BLM access roads with variable conditions. High-clearance vehicle recommended but not required in most seasons. Washboard surface typical. Impassable during heavy rain or snow.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and turnarounds along BLM access road. Capacity varies from 2-6 vehicles per area. No developed parking facilities or amenities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA guidelines. No commercial collection or sale of specimens. No motorized excavation or powered tools. Surface collection and hand tools only. Do not trespass on private claims or active mining operations if marked.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Respect private claims and mining operations
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Prescott, 29 miles northwest. Full services including gas, food, lodging, medical, and rockhounding supply shops.
accessibility_notes: Relatively accessible for most hikers. Uneven rocky terrain with loose stone underfoot requires sturdy footwear and careful footing. Moderate uphill walking to reach best collecting zones. Not suitable for wheelchairs or severe mobility limitations. No developed trails or rest areas.

faq.question: Can you find gold at Rich Hill near Prescott?
faq.answer: Yes, Rich Hill is part of Arizona's historic Hassayampa Mining District where Proterozoic granitic rocks host quartz veins carrying fine gold. Surface specimens are accessible to rockhounds - expect visible gold flakes in fractured quartz samples under magnification. Most hobby collectors find small quantities suitable for personal collections.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at Rich Hill in Arizona?
faq.answer: No permit is required. Rich Hill sits on public BLM land in Yavapai County where recreational mineral collection is allowed under FLPMA guidelines. You do not need to pay any fee or obtain advance permission to collect on designated BLM acres.

faq.question: What is the daily collection limit at Rich Hill, Arizona?
faq.answer: The limit is 25 pounds per day per person under FLPMA rules for BLM land. This applies to all gem and mineral specimens combined. Commercial collection or sale is not permitted - only personal use collecting is allowed.

faq.question: What tools do I need to rockhound for gold and quartz at Rich Hill?
faq.answer: Bring a small hammer and cold chisel for breaking samples from quartz outcrops, a 10x magnifying glass to spot visible gold in quartz, a sturdy geology pick, and a collection bag. Avoid motorized tools - hand tools only on BLM land. A GPS device or smartphone map app is helpful for navigation on unmarked terrain.

faq.question: When is the best time to visit Rich Hill for rockhounding near Prescott?
faq.answer: October through April are ideal months. Summer heat exceeds 85 degrees F regularly at this 5,196-foot elevation. Winter and shoulder season temperatures make full-day collecting comfortable. Avoid mid-summer visits unless you start very early and finish by afternoon.

faq.question: Is Rich Hill a good beginner rockhounding site in Arizona?
faq.answer: Yes, Rich Hill is beginner-friendly. Access is straightforward via unpaved BLM roads, parking is informal but adequate, and the terrain is moderately sloped rather than technical. No permit complexity. Kids ages 8+ can participate safely with supervision. Surface specimens are visible and accessible without advanced prospecting skills.

meta_title: Rich Hill Rockhounding - Arizona BLM Gold Collecting
meta_description: Find gold and quartz at Rich Hill near Prescott, Arizona. BLM public land, no permit required. 25 lb/day limit. Surface specimens for rockhounders.
